docs.title: The BibbleVM Instruction Set
docs.text: Disclaimer: I got really bored of writing 10 versions of the same arithmetic instructions, so I had an AI do it for me. This means that there could be some writing inconsistencies, but the overall logic of them should be accurate. Readers are encouraged to suggest edits to improve clarity and consistency for these instructions.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Even though this terminates the BibbleVM, it doesn't necessarily terminate the host process running the it.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Executes an implementation-defined VM routine identified by code.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Intended for use by standard library implementations. Not typically used in user code.

docs.title: Operation
docs.text: Execute a implementation-defined VM trap routine if accumulator is 0

docs.title: Description
docs.text: If the value in accumulator is equal to 0, executes an implementation-defined VM routine identified by code. Otherwise, does nothing.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Intended for use by standard library implementations. Not typically used in user code.

docs.title: Operation
docs.text: Execute a implementation-defined VM trap routine if accumulator isn't 0

docs.title: Description
docs.text: If the value in accumulator isn't equal to 0, executes an implementation-defined VM routine identified by code. Otherwise, does nothing.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: Intended for use by standard library implementations. Not typically used in user code.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: When a debugger is attached, causes a breakpoint. Otherwise does nothing.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit signed integers. b is popped from the stack and added to a directly in the accumulator. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit signed integers. b is popped from the stack and subtracted from a directly in the accumulator. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit signed integers. b is popped from the stack and a is multiplied by it directly in the accumulator. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit signed integers. b is popped from the stack and a is divided by it directly in the accumulator. Division of the most negative 64-bit value by -1 produces a result that cannot be represented. This case is undefined behavior, but does not raise a runtime error.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: If the divisor is 0, causes a runtime error.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit signed integers. b is popped from the stack. he result is a - (a / b) * b and is moved to the accumulator. Applying modulo to the most negative 64-bit value with a divisor of -1 is undefined due to the underlying division step, but does not raise a runtime error.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: If the divisor is 0, causes a runtime error.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack and the accumulator is set to the result of bitwise AND (conjunction) of a and b.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack and the accumulator is set to the result of bitwise OR of a and b.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack and the accumulator is set to the result of bitwise exclusive OR of a and b.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack and the value in accumulator is shifted left by s bit positions, where s is the value of the low 6 bits of b.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack and the value in accumulator is shifted right by s bit positions, where s is the value of the low 6 bits of b.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The value in the accumulator is set to the negation of a. Negation is the same as subtraction from 0.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The value in the accumulator is set to the bitwise complement of a (all bits inverted).

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is a + b and is moved to the accumulator. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is a - b and is moved to the accumulator. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is a * b and is moved to the accumulator. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is a / b and is moved to the accumulator. Division of the most negative 64-bit value by -1 produces a result that cannot be represented. This case is undefined behavior, but does not raise a runtime error.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: If the divisor is 0, causes a runtime error.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is a - (a / b) * b and is moved to the accumulator. Applying modulo to the most negative 64-bit value with a divisor of -1 is undefined due to the underlying division step, but does not raise a runtime error.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: If the divisor is 0, causes a runtime error. None

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is the bitwise AND (conjunction) of a and b and is moved to the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is the bitwise OR of a and b and is moved to the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is the bitwise exclusive OR of a and b and is moved to the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is a shifted left by s bit positions, where s is the value of the low 6 bits of b and is moved to the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is a shifted left by s bit positions, where s is the value of the low 6 bits of b and is moved to the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is a + b and is pushed onto the stack. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is a - b and is pushed onto the stack. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

Division of the most negative 64-bit value by -1 produces a result that cannot be represented. This case is undefined behavior, but does not raise a runtime error.

Applying modulo to the most negative 64-bit value with a divisor of -1 is undefined due to the underlying division step, but does not raise a runtime error.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is bitwise AND (conjunction) of a and b and is pushed onto the stack.

docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is bitwise OR of a and b and is pushed onto the stack.

docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. The values are popped from the stack. The result is bitwise exclusive OR of a and b and is pushed onto the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is set to the negation of a. Negation is the same as subtraction from 0.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is set to the bitwise complement of a (all bits inverted).

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The value in the accumulator is increased by the 32-bit immediate value. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The value in the accumulator is decreased by the 32-bit immediate value. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The value in the accumulator is multiplied by the 32-bit immediate value. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The accumulator is divided by the 32-bit immediate value. Division of the most negative 64-bit value by -1 produces a result that cannot be represented. This case is undefined behavior, but does not raise a runtime error.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: If the divisor is 0, causes a runtime error.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The accumulator is set to the result of a - (a / value) * value. Applying modulo to the most negative 64-bit value with a divisor of -1 is undefined due to the underlying division step, but does not raise a runtime error.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: If the divisor is 0, causes a runtime error.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The value in the accumulator is set to the result of bitwise AND (conjunction) of a and the 32-bit immediate value.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The value in the accumulator is set to the result of bitwise OR of a and the 32-bit immediate value.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The value in the accumulator is set to the result of bitwise exclusive OR of a and the 32-bit immediate value.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The value in the accumulator is shifted left by s bit positions, where s is the value of the low 6 bits of the 32-bit immediate value.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The value in the accumulator is shifted right by s bit positions, where s is the value of the low 6 bits of the 32-bit immediate value.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is increased by the 32-bit immediate value. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is decreased by the 32-bit immediate value. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is multiplied by the 32-bit immediate value. Overflow behavior is undefined, but never results in runtime errors.

docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is divided by the 32-bit immediate value. Division of the most negative 64-bit value by -1 produces a result that cannot be represented. This case is undefined behavior, but does not raise a runtime error.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: If the divisor is 0, causes a runtime error.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is replaced with the result of a - (a / value) * value. Applying modulo to the most negative 64-bit value with a divisor of -1 is undefined due to the underlying division step, but does not raise a runtime error.

docs.title: Errors
docs.text: If the divisor is 0, causes a runtime error.

docs.title: Operation
docs.text: Bitwise AND top of stack with immediate

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is set to the result of bitwise AND (conjunction) of a and the 32-bit immediate value.

docs.title: Operation
docs.text: Bitwise OR top of stack with immediate

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is set to the result of bitwise OR of a and the 32-bit immediate value.

docs.title: Operation
docs.text: Bitwise XOR top of stack with immediate

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is set to the result of bitwise exclusive OR of a and the 32-bit immediate value.

docs.title: Operation
docs.text: Shift left top of stack by immediate

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is shifted left by s bit positions, where s is the value of the low 6 bits of the 32-bit immediate value.

docs.title: Operation
docs.text: Shift right top of stack by immediate

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The top value of the stack is shifted right by s bit positions, where s is the value of the low 6 bits of the 32-bit immediate value.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. b is popped from the stack and added to the accumulator a.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. b is popped from the stack and subtracted from the accumulator a.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. b is popped from the stack and multiplied with the accumulator a.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. b is popped from the stack and the accumulator a is divided by b. If b is 0.0, the result is IEEE 754-defined (±Inf or NaN).

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. The values are popped from the stack. The result a + b is moved to the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. The values are popped from the stack. The result a - b is moved to the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. The values are popped from the stack. The result a * b is moved to the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. The values are popped from the stack. The result a / b is moved to the accumulator. If b is 0.0, the result is IEEE 754-defined (±Inf or NaN).

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. The values are popped from the stack and their sum is pushed back.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. The values are popped from the stack and the difference is pushed back.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. The values are popped from the stack and the product is pushed back.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floating-point values. The values are popped from the stack and the quotient is pushed back. If b is 0.0, the result is IEEE 754-defined (±Inf or NaN).

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit floating-point value. The value in the accumulator is negated.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Adds a 32-bit floating-point immediate value to the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Subtracts a 32-bit floating-point immediate value from the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Multiplies the accumulator by a 32-bit floating-point immediate value.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Divides the accumulator by a 32-bit floating-point immediate value. If value is 0.0, the result is IEEE 754-defined (±Inf or NaN).

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Pops the top float from the stack, adds a 32-bit immediate value, and pushes the result.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Pops the top float from the stack, subtracts a 32-bit immediate value, and pushes the result.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Pops the top float from the stack, multiplies by a 32-bit immediate value, and pushes the result.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Pops the top float from the stack, divides by a 32-bit immediate value, and pushes the result. If value is 0.0, the result is IEEE 754-defined (±Inf or NaN).

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack. The result is a == b and is pushed to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack. The result is a != b and is pushed to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack. The result is a < b and is pushed to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack. The result is a > b and is pushed to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack. The result is a <= b and is pushed to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit integers. b is popped from the stack. The result is a >= b and is pushed to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Both a and b must be 64-bit floats. b is popped from the stack. The result is a >= b and is pushed to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The result is a == 0 and is pushed to the stack.

docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The result is a != 0 and is pushed to the stack.

docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The result is a < 0 and is pushed to the stack.

docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The result is a > 0 and is pushed to the stack.

docs.text: a must be a 64-bit integer. The result is a >= 0 and is pushed to the stack.

docs.text: a must be a 64-bit float. The result is a != 0.0 and is pushed to the stack.

docs.text: a must be a 64-bit float. The result is a < 0.0 and is pushed to the stack.

docs.text: a must be a 64-bit float. The result is a > 0.0 and is pushed to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: acc is the current 64-bit value in the accumulator. acc is pushed onto the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: sp is the current 64-bit value in the stack pointer register. sp is pushed onto the stack.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: The stack pointer will never be a real memory pointer in sandbox mode. Other than that, its value is implementation-defined.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The topmost stack value is popped and moved into the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The topmost stack value is popped and moved into the stack pointer register.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Pops n elements from the stack and discards their values.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Loads an 8-bit immediate value and moves it into the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Loads an 32-bit immediate value and moves it into the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Loads an 64-bit immediate value and moves it into the accumulator.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Loads an 8-bit immediate value and pushes it to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Loads an 32-bit immediate value and pushes it to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Loads an 64-bit immediate value and pushes it to the stack.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The way this instruction loads a value depends on whether index is positive or negative. If index is positive (>= 0), load from the current frame's stack base plus index. If index is negative (< 0), load from the stack pointer register minus the absolute value of index. For example, a given index of -1 would load the topmost stack value, and a given index of 0 would load the first stack value.

docs.title: Operation
docs.text: Load stack value and push to stack

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The way this instruction loads a value depends on whether index is positive or negative. If index is positive (>= 0), load from the current frame’s stack base plus index. If index is negative (< 0), load from the stack pointer register minus the absolute value of index. For example, an index of -1 would load the topmost stack value and push it, while an index of 0 would load the first stack value and push it.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The way this instruction stores a value depends on whether index is positive or negative. If index is positive (>= 0), store to the current frame’s stack base plus index. If index is negative (< 0), store to the stack pointer register minus the absolute value of index. For example, an index of -1 stores the accumulator to the topmost stack slot, while an index of 0 stores to the first slot.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: The way this instruction stores a value depends on whether index is positive or negative. If index is positive (>= 0), store to the current frame’s stack base plus index. If index is negative (< 0), store to the stack pointer register minus the absolute value of index before the value is popped. For example, an index of -1 stores the popped value to the topmost slot before popping, while an index of 0 stores to the first slot.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Unconditionally increments the program counter by branch as a signed integer.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Increments the program counter by branch as a signed integer if the value in the accumulator is equal to 0.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Increments the program counter by branch as a signed integer if the value in the accumulator is not equal to 0.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: target is an address pointing to a CallEntry within the data section. The stack must contain argc values. For simplicity, we will refer to the addressed CallEntry as 'the function.' This does not mean that a CallEntry always has to be a function symbol. If the function is not yet resolved, its module and entry point are resolved and cached for future use. If the function is not native, the argc values are popped from the stack. A new stack frame is created on the current thread. The argc values are pushed in reverse order so that they appear in the same order on the new frame as they were in the previous frame. The new frame is then made the current, and the program counter is set to the specified entry of the function. If the function is native, the argc values are popped from the stack and are passed as parameters to the implementation of the function. The code is executed in an implementation-dependent manner. When the platform-dependent code returns, the return value storage is moved to the accumulator regardless of the functions return type. In the case of a void function, the accumulator will contain garbage data.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Works like the CALL instruction, but with a wide argc operand.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Works like the CALL_EX instruction, but the target address is determined at runtime. The target address is taken from the low 32 bits of the accumulator and treated as an unsigned 32-bit integer.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Works like the CALL instruction, but with a narrow target operand.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Works like the CALL instruction, but with a narrow target operand and wide argc operand.

docs.title: Description
docs.text: Any values on the current stack frame are discarded. The interpreter then returns control to the caller by updating the program counter to a saved value.
